content.text: Verifiable Credentials

Verifiable Credentials (VCs) are the portable, cryptographically-signed digital credentials at the core of the W3C decentralized identity model. Microsoft Entra Verified ID implements VCs for enterprise use — enabling organizations to issue tamper-proof digital badges, employment cards, academic diplomas, or any identity claim that other parties can verify without contacting the original issuer.

attrs.alt: Verifiable Credential lifecycle: Issue, Store in wallet, Present as VP, Verify signature and status

content.text: The W3C VC Data Model

A Verifiable Credential is a JSON-LD document conforming to the W3C Verifiable Credentials Data Model 1.1 specification. Every credential has three mandatory components: a set of claims about the subject (credentialSubject), the issuer's DID, and a cryptographic proof. Entra Verified ID uses the JsonWebSignature2020 proof type backed by ECDSA P-256 keys.
Credential Manifest — Rules and Display

Every credential type in Entra Verified ID is defined by a credential manifest with two parts: a rules file that specifies how claims are sourced, and a display file that controls how the credential renders in Microsoft Authenticator.
Rules File (rules.json)
Display File (display.json)
Attestation Types
The rules.json attestation block defines where claims come from. Entra Verified ID supports three attestation sources, which can be mixed in the same credential.
Attestation Type
How It Works
Best For
idTokenHints
Your issuance API passes claims directly in the createIssuanceRequest payload
Employee credentials where you already have the claims in your HR system
idTokens
Holder signs in with an OIDC IdP during issuance; claims come from the id_token
Social or educational credentials where the holder authenticates to a third-party IdP
presentations
Holder presents an existing VC during issuance; claims are derived from it
Cross-credential issuance (e.g., use a government ID VC to issue a bank account VC)
Credential Status — StatusList2021

Entra Verified ID implements the W3C StatusList2021 specification for revocation. The issuer maintains a status list credential — a compressed bitstring where each bit represents one issued credential. A bit value of 0 means valid; 1 means revoked. Verifiers fetch the status list and check the bit at the credential's statusListIndex.
The status list is a public document — it reveals only bit positions, not credential holder identities. An adversary who obtains the list learns nothing about who holds which credential.
Verifiable Presentations (VP) — Holder Proof

When a holder presents credentials to a verifier, they wrap them in a Verifiable Presentation. The VP is signed with the holder's own DID key and includes a nonce (challenge) provided by the verifier, preventing replay attacks. The verifier checks both the issuer's proof on the VC and the holder's proof on the VP.
Creating and Managing Credential Types (PowerShell)

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'VerifiableCredential.ReadWrite.All'

# List existing credential contracts (types) in the tenant
Invoke-MgGraphRequest `
    -Method GET `
    -Uri 'https://verifiedid.did.msidentity.com/v1.0/<tenant-id>/verifiableCredentials/contracts' |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Format-Table id, displayName, issuerId

# Delete a credential contract (removes type; does NOT revoke issued credentials)
$contractId = 'VerifiedEmployee'
Invoke-MgGraphRequest `
    -Method DELETE `
    -Uri "https://verifiedid.did.msidentity.com/v1.0/<tenant-id>/verifiableCredentials/contracts/$contractId"
Write-Host 'Contract deleted.'

# List all issued credentials for a contract (for revocation audit)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest `
    -Method GET `
    -Uri "https://verifiedid.did.msidentity.com/v1.0/<tenant-id>/verifiableCredentials/contracts/$contractId/issuances" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Format-Table id, issuanceDate, expirationDate
Claims Indexing and Search

When you mark a claim as indexed: true in the rules.json, Entra Verified ID indexes that claim value and allows you to search for issued credentials by claim value. This is useful for operations like 'find all credentials issued to employees in the Engineering department' or 'revoke all credentials for a terminated employee by name'.

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'VerifiableCredential.ReadWrite.All'

# Search issued credentials by an indexed claim value
# Only works for claims marked as indexed:true in rules.json
$contractId  = 'VerifiedEmployee'
$searchClaim = 'department'
$searchValue = 'Engineering'

$searchBody = @{
    claimValue = $searchValue
    claimName  = $searchClaim
} | ConvertTo-Json

Invoke-MgGraphRequest `
    -Method POST `
    -Uri "https://verifiedid.did.msidentity.com/v1.0/<tenant-id>/verifiableCredentials/contracts/$contractId/search" `
    -Body $searchBody `
    -ContentType 'application/json' |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
    Format-Table id, issuanceDate
VC vs Traditional Identity Tokens
Property
JWT / OAuth2 Access Token
Verifiable Credential
Issuer
Identity Provider (IdP) — centralized
Issuer DID — decentralized, self-sovereign
Lifetime
Short-lived (minutes to hours)
Long-lived (days to years), with explicit expiry
Storage
Application memory or session
Holder's wallet (Authenticator), persistent
Verification
Token introspection or JWKS endpoint call to IdP
Cryptographic proof check using public DID key — no IdP call needed
Revocation
Short expiry + token blacklist at IdP
StatusList2021 bit flip — verifier checks status list URL
Privacy
IdP logs every validation
Verifier checks directly — issuer does not know when/where credential is presented
Portability
Bound to issuing IdP's ecosystem
Portable across any verifier that accepts the W3C VC standard
End-to-End Issuance with idTokens Attestation

The idTokens attestation type is used when the credential claims come from a third-party OIDC provider that the holder must authenticate with during issuance. This is common for educational credentials (holder authenticates to their university's IdP) or government credentials (holder authenticates to a government portal).
Configuring idTokens in rules.json
Issuance Request with idTokens (no claims in payload)
Linked Domain Verification

Microsoft Entra Verified ID requires that the issuer's DID be linked to a verified domain. This creates a bidirectional trust anchor: the DID document references the domain, and the domain hosts a well-known file that references the DID. Without this link, the consent screen in Authenticator displays a warning.

The Entra Verified ID portal generates and hosts the did-configuration.json automatically when you verify your domain. You do not need to manually create this file for did:web domains configured via the portal.
Bulk Revocation (PowerShell)

When an employee leaves the organization, you want to revoke all VCs issued to them. Since employee ID is an indexed claim, you can search for all issued credentials with that employee ID and revoke them in a loop.

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'VerifiableCredential.ReadWrite.All'

$tenantId   = '<your-tenant-id>'
$contractId = 'VerifiedEmployee'
$baseUri    = "https://verifiedid.did.msidentity.com/v1.0/$tenantId/verifiableCredentials"

# Step 1: Search for all credentials issued to a specific employee
# Requires 'employeeId' to be marked indexed:true in rules.json
$searchResult = Invoke-MgGraphRequest `
    -Method POST `
    -Uri "$baseUri/contracts/$contractId/search" `
    -Body (@{ claimName = 'employeeId'; claimValue = 'E54321' } | ConvertTo-Json) `
    -ContentType 'application/json'

$credentials = $searchResult.value
Write-Host "Found $($credentials.Count) credential(s) to revoke."

# Step 2: Revoke each credential
foreach ($cred in $credentials) {
    Invoke-MgGraphRequest `
        -Method POST `
        -Uri "$baseUri/contracts/$contractId/revoke" `
        -Body (@{ id = $cred.id } | ConvertTo-Json) `
        -ContentType 'application/json'
    Write-Host "Revoked: $($cred.id)"
}
Write-Host 'Bulk revocation complete.'
Verifying a Presented VP (Python)

The Entra Verified ID service handles cryptographic verification for you via the callback. However, your application's callback handler must validate the API key, check the requestStatus, and extract and trust only the claims from credentialsData. Here is a production-ready callback handler in Python (Flask).
KQL: Tracking VC Issuance and Verification Events
Issuance Success Rate

// Track VC issuance success vs. failure rate by credential type
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where Category == 'VerifiedId'
| where OperationName has 'Issue'
| extend CredType = tostring(AdditionalDetails[0].value)
| summarize
    Issued  = countif(Result == 'success'),
    Failed  = countif(Result != 'success')
    by CredType, bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Revocation Events

// Audit all credential revocations
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(90d)
| where Category == 'VerifiedId'
| where OperationName has 'Revoke'
| extend RevokedBy  = InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName
| extend CredType   = tostring(AdditionalDetails[0].value)
| extend CredId     = tostring(TargetResources[0].id)
| project TimeGenerated, CredType, CredId, RevokedBy, Result
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Common Configuration Errors
Error
Root Cause
Fix
'manifest not found' during issuance
Manifest URL returns 404 or is behind auth
Ensure the manifest JSON is publicly readable at the manifest URL with no authentication required
Claims missing in issued VC
Required claim not passed in idTokenHints 'claims' payload
Check rules.json required fields; ensure all required claims are in the createIssuanceRequest payload
'linked domain not verified' warning
did-configuration.json not present or DID not verified in portal
Complete domain verification in the Entra Verified ID portal before issuing credentials
Credential not revocable
credentialStatus block absent from issued VC
Ensure rules.json does not disable revocation; portal setting must have 'Enable credential status' checked
'indexed search returns no results'
Claim not marked indexed:true in rules.json at issuance time
Re-issue credentials with the updated rules.json; previously issued credentials use the mapping that was active at issuance
Verification callback never fires
Callback URL not reachable from Microsoft's outbound IPs
Use a public HTTPS endpoint; test with ngrok for local dev; add Microsoft Azure IP ranges to firewall allowlist
Summary
Verifiable Credentials deliver tamper-proof, privacy-preserving digital identity claims using open W3C standards implemented by Microsoft Entra Verified ID.
VCs are JSON-LD documents signed with the issuer's DID private key; the proof block contains a compact JWS using ECDSA P-256.
Credential manifests define the claim schema (rules.json) and the wallet UI (display.json); both are hosted at a public URL.
Three attestation sources: idTokenHints (issuer provides claims), idTokens (holder authenticates to IdP), presentations (holder provides existing VC).
StatusList2021 enables revocation via a public bitstring — verifiers check the bit at the credential's statusListIndex.
Verifiable Presentations wrap VCs with a holder proof and a verifier-supplied nonce — proving possession and preventing replay attacks.
Indexed claims enable server-side search of issued credentials by claim value — useful for bulk revocation.
Unlike OAuth tokens, VCs are long-lived, portable across verifiers, and do not require the issuer to be contacted at verification time.
